coredata.dc:title: Solvent-free preparation of high synthetic yield carbon dots from sodium ligninsulfonate for on-site detection of formaldehyde in the environment
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Fang
Zhang, Ting
Xing, Haoming
Wang, Linfan
Hu, Houwen
Zhang, Yihao
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: Formaldehyde (FA), as a widespread toxic pollutant in cars, furniture and construction materials, has been implicated in hazardous health effects on the human body. This paper presents a green and large-scale preparation of carbon dots (CDs) using sodium ligninsulfonate and o-phenylenediamine through a solvent-free reaction method. The prepared CDs exhibit a synthetic yield of 43 % and show green fluorescence. In addition, the CDs can be employed as an ideal fluorescent FA probe, exhibiting excellent linearity within the range of 0–50 μM (R2 = 0.998, limit of detection (LOD) = 79 nM). By taking advantage of the obvious color changes in detection, the droplet detection system is investigated for on-site visual detection of indoor FA gas. This work not only contributes to the efficient utilization of agricultural waste but also represents a significant process in the development of FA on-site detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solvent-free
Luminescence
Sensors
Formaldehyde detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs (Inset: HR-TEM image). (b) The size distribution of CDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of SL, OPD and CDs. (d) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs. (e) Emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (f) The CIE coordinate of CDs.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of CDs with increasing FA concentrations. (b) The relationship of F/F 0 and the concentration of FA. Inset is the linear relationship. (c) The selectivity for FA detection. (d) Images of CDs with various concentrations of FA under daylight and UV light. (e) The diagram of detection mechanism. (f) Color photo and (g) fluorescence responses of the CDs/PVA droplets with different concentrations of FA gas and for different times.
Scheme of synthesizing CDs and the visual detection of FA.

head.ce:title: Solvent-free preparation of high synthetic yield carbon dots from sodium ligninsulfonate for on-site detection of formaldehyde in the environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Engineering College, Ningbo Polytechnic, Ningbo 315800, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical Engineering College, Ningbo Polytechnic, Ningbo 315800, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Formaldehyde (FA), as a widespread toxic pollutant in cars, furniture and construction materials, has been implicated in hazardous health effects on the human body. This paper presents a green and large-scale preparation of carbon dots (CDs) using sodium ligninsulfonate and o-phenylenediamine through a solvent-free reaction method. The prepared CDs exhibit a synthetic yield of 43 % and show green fluorescence. In addition, the CDs can be employed as an ideal fluorescent FA probe, exhibiting excellent linearity within the range of 0–50 μM (R2 = 0.998, limit of detection (LOD) = 79 nM). By taking advantage of the obvious color changes in detection, the droplet detection system is investigated for on-site visual detection of indoor FA gas. This work not only contributes to the efficient utilization of agricultural waste but also represents a significant process in the development of FA on-site detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Formaldehyde (FA) is one of the synthetic materials in modern chemical industry and widely used in the production of plastics, glues, construction materials, household products and pharmaceuticals.[1,2] However, FA poses a significant threat as an indoor air pollutant emanating from buildings and decorative materials.[3] Long-term exposure of human body to FA environment will lead to serious damage to the central nervous system, blood, immune system and respiratory system.[2,3] According to the China’s National Standard, the maximum allowable level of FA in indoor air is 0.1 mg m−3, and the maximum permissible limit in potable water is 0.9 mg L-1.[3] Therefore, it is necessary to exploit a rapid, convenient and highly sensitive method for detecting free FA in indoor air.

Electrochemical analysis, spectroscopy, high performance liquid chromatography and fluorescence technique are common methods for FA detection.[2,3] Among them, the fluorescence technique stands out as a reliable method due to the fast response, good sensitivity and excellent selectivity.[1,3] Recently, fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a new nanomaterial that has attracted intense interests due to its good photostability, excellent optical properties and easy synthesis from extensive carbon sources,[4,5] which has led to successful applications in the detection of metal ions, tetracyclines, amines, pesticides, etc.[4–6] Meanwhile, the method of synthesizing CDs using the natural precursor green has received extensive attention.[5,6] Nevertheless, there are a few reports about FA detection by bio-friendly CDs. Qu et al. reported N, P-CDs for FA detection.[1] Chen et al. developed a ratiometric fluorescence probe based on CDs to detect FA.[6] However, those probes only detect FA in the solution and cannot realize on-site visual detection of FA gas. Currently, many FA detection instruments are typically employed to detect indoor FA gas, which exhibits the disadvantages of an expensive price, cumbersome operation and long time consumption.[2] Therefore, it is of great values to design a portable and intuitive detection method based on CDs for on-site visual detection of indoor FA gas.

In this work, the sodium ligninsulfonate derived CDs are obtained through a solvent-free reaction method (SFRM) with a high yield of 43 %, exhibiting a strong emission at 532 nm (Scheme 1 ). The CDs exhibit a linear response to FA within the range of 0–50 μM (LOD: 79 nM). In addition, the visual on-site detection of indoor FA gas is realized using the CDs-droplet detection system. This work supports the development of CDs derived from the sodium ligninsulfonate on a large scale and simplifies the method for indoor FA gas detection.

Sodium ligninsulfonate (SL), o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and formaldehyde standard solution are purchased from Sinopharm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images are obtained from a JEM-ARM300F. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra are acquired by a Nicolet iS50 spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra are recorded on a RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer.

The CDs are prepared by a SFRM strategy. Firstly, 1 mmol SL and 1 mmol OPD are ground in the mortar for 5 min. Then the powders are stored in a Teflon reactor for 8 h at 180 °C. When the temperature of the solution is reduced to room temperature, the water is used to dissolve the product. Then the centrifugation of 8000 rpm for 10 min and a 0.22 μm filter membrane are used to remove the impurities. Finally, the powder of CDs is obtained by freeze-drying.
The detection method is shown in Supporting information.

The CDs are easily synthesized through a situ SFRM strategy by directly heating of SL and OPD, which exhibits high yield of 43 %. As the temperature increases to 180 °C, the hydrogen bonds of SL begin to fracture, leading to the collapse of the lignin struction. Consequently, the SL is further cleaved into small fragments (volatile aromatic products, oligomers) due to thermal effect.[3] In the solvent-free environment, OPD is more easily oxidized, forming OPD dimer/polymer. This process generates more active sites, enhancing effective collisions in the reaction process. Then the OPD dimer/polymer further polymerizes and carbonizes with the small fragments, resulting in the formation of a carbon core. Finally, the CDs are successfully synthesized. More importantly, considering the situ SFRM strategy, gram-scale preparation of these products can be readily achieved in the laboratory, presenting great potential for massive applications in industrial.

Fig. 1 (a) reveals the uniform distribution and monodispersity of CDs from TEM results. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image shows a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm for CDs, corresponding to the graphene (100) plane.[2] Their diameters are distributed in the range of 1–5 nm, with an average diameter of 2.7 nm (Fig. 1(b)). Moreover, the functional groups and the structure of SL, OPD and CDs are identified by FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1(c)). It is clearly observed that two similar peaks appear at 3177–3400 cm−1 and 1431–1490 cm−1, attributed to O-H/N-H and CC stretching vibration.[5] Meanwhile, compared with the SL, the CDs exhibit two new peaks at 2843–2954 cm−1 and 1736 cm−1, which are ascribed to the in-plane deformation vibration of sp2 C-H and C = O groups.[4] In addition, the stretching vibrations of –NH2 (1637 cm−1), C-N (1259 cm−1), C-O-C (1052–1166 cm−1) are observed in CDs, indicating the preservation of the natural carbon skeleton structure of SL and the successful doping of N into the framework of CDs during the synthesis process.[3].

As shown in Fig. 1(d), the peaks at 203 nm and 236 nm are associated with the π-π* transition of CC, and other two peaks around 273 nm and 293 nm are ascribed to the n-π* transition of CO/CN.[2,4] The calculated energy gap of CDs is 2.38 eV from the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S1). The CDs exhibit green fluorescence at 532 nm under the excitation of 420 nm (quantum yield = 31 %). The photos of CDs solution presented in the inset of Fig. 1(d), reveal colorless under sunlight and green emission under UV lamp. As the excitation wavelength varies from 370 to 470 nm, the CDs exhibit an excitation wavelength-independent characteristic (Fig. 1(e)). The calculated CIE coordinates of CDs is (0.34, 0.57) (Fig. 1(f)).

Here the response capacity of CDs toward FA is evaluated. With the FA concentration increasing from 0 to 150 μM, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually enhances and shifts to 571 nm (Fig. 2 (a)). Meanwhile, the relationship between F/F 0 (F and F 0 is the intensity of CDs with and without FA) and FA concentration exhibits a good linearity from 0 to 50 μM with R2 = 0.998 (LOD = 79 nM), demonstrating the great potential of CDs as a fluorescent probe for FA detection (Fig. 2(b)). In addition, the CDs exhibit excellent sensitivity and selectivity for FA detection with the different metal ions, anions, methanol and formic acid (Fig. 2(c)). With the increase of FA concentration, the solution undergoes an obvious color change from colorless to light yellow under sunlight and green to yellow under UV light, which can be easily observed by naked eyes (Fig. 2(d)). For the sensing mechanism, the formation of Schiff bases via the amine reaction between the amine groups on CDs and FA is hypothesized to be the primary cause. This reaction causes the aggregation of CDs, resulting in enhanced fluorescence intensity and decreased band gaps of CDs (Fig. 2(e)). The detailed description is expressed in Supporting information.

In consideration of the visual fluorescence response to FA, a droplet detection system based on polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and CDs has been developed for the actual indoor detection of FA gas. For this detection system, it just needs to brush the PVA/CDs gel on the cleaned solid surface to form the droplet, possessing low cost, simple preparation process, rapid response and visual detection process. As demonstrated in Fig. 2(f-g), with the increase of FA gas concentration, the droplets change from colorless to light yellow, and the fluorescence shifts from green to yellow under UV light. It is clearly shown that the color is observable within 1 min when the FA gas concentration is ≥20 ppm. Meanwhile, the more obvious color changes become apparent with longer response times (2–3 min). The liquid phase of droplet detection system enhances the contact area between the droplets and FA gas, facilitating the adsorption of FA gas and improving the sensitivity and the response speed. Besides, the droplets can form solid films after the natural evaporation of the solvent, thus allowing for prolonged observation. In summary, the droplet detection system exhibits great potential for on-site visual detection of FA gas in indoor environmental pollution.

In conclusion, the SL derived CDs are developed via the SFRM, achieving a high yield of 43 % with green fluorescence. Meanwhile, the CDs show an excellent fluorescent response to FA and exhibit a linear range of 0–50 μM (LOD: 79 nm). The solution undergoes obvious color change under sunlight and UV light. Furthermore, a droplet detection system based on PVA and CDs for the visual on-site detection of indoor FA gas is developed. Our work provides a promising environmentally friendly method for the large-scale preparation of CDs from agricultural residue and broadens the potential application of indoor FA gas detection through the droplet detection.

Fang Ma: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Ting Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Haoming Xing: Methodology, Investigation. Linfan Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Houwen Hu: Investigation. Yihao Zhang: Investigation. Da Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by project from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084) and Talent-introduction training special project of NBPT (NZ23RC02).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2024.136223.
